Chapter VIII: The Beiyang Stratocracy and the Ming Restoration

The dawn of the 20th century witnessed a profound transformation in the political landscape of the Middle Kingdom. Amidst the turbulence of changing leadership and ideological struggles, the Beiyang Government emerged as the central force in Chinese politics. Commanding authority from 1912 until the mid-1940s, this stratocratic regime would shepherd the nation through a period of intermittent civil strife and international turmoil, all while serving the ultimate goal of reviving the imperial glory of the Ming—China’s last dynasty under the Han Chinese system.

Established as the governing body of the Republic of China after the collapse of the Qing dynasty, the Beiyang Government transitioned through various incarnations. Initially, it functioned as a semi-democratic entity with visions of parliamentary governance but gradually devolved into an authoritarian rule with militaristic and semi-totalitarian tendencies.

The period of the Republic (1912-1915) was brief and fraught with division, leading to the ascendancy of Yuan Shikai who sought to further centralize power. His ambition culminated in the creation of the short-lived Empire of China on December 12, 1915, making himself emperor. Yet, his regime proved unpopular and ephemeral, ending swiftly after his death on June 6, 1916, under dubious circumstances.

In the wake of Yuan's demise, Sun Yat-sen, the venerated revolutionary leader, assumed the office of Great President, instilling renewed hope for a republican government. However, the ideals of a democratic China were soon overshadowed by a prevailing sentiment among the Han Chinese elite: the restoration of a Huaren monarchy. It was this drive that realigned the nation’s destiny with the ideals of its imperial past.

On January 1, 1916, the dream of restoration was realized when Zhu Yuxun, a descendant of the Ming dynasty's royal house, ascended to the Dragon Throne in Beijing, enthroned as Emperor Hongxian. Sun Yat-sen presided as the Prime Minister, playing a pivotal role in harmonizing the government’s structure with the revived monarchy.

In the years that followed, the Beiyang Government, now reborn as a regency, would spearhead the consolidation of the Ming restoration. It abandoned claims to Outer Mongolia, Inner Mongolia, Tibet, Xinjiang, and Manchuria, seeking stability and international recognition within the heartland of historical China.

The Beiyang Government maintained the status of China as a protectorate of the Empire of Japan—a relationship forged during desperate times of Chinese internal and external challenges. This strategic alliance saw political marriages intended to merge the remnants of the Yuan dynasty with the newly restored Ming, promoting harmony among the former ruling elites.

On the fringes of the empire, various former territories embraced their independence following the tumultuous fall of the Qing. In Outer Mongolia, the Bogd Khanate, a theocratic Buddhist monarchy, prospered under the Jebtsundamba Khutuktus, esteemed Gelug lamas who ruled as Bogd Khagans.

Meanwhile, Taiwan, once a Qing territory, became home to the descendants of the Kingdom of Tungning's former authorities. To mitigate the anti-Japanese sentiments pervasive among the Chinese settlers and integrate indigenous peoples and Joseon migrants, Japan encouraged the pro-Ming loyalists to move to the Chinese heartland, thereby fortifying the re-established imperial order.

Thus, those once-subjugated now found themselves in positions of influence, their dynastic loyalties realigned to align with contemporary geopolitical realities. In this intricate web of shifting allegiances, the Beiyang Government—ever pragmatic—retained the fabric of authority through its careful facilitation of the Ming Restoration.

In summary, the Beiyang epoch was marked by a complex dance of military power, benevolent autocracy, and eventual subservience to the Japanese Empire. Still, through strategic marriages and political maneuvering, the Ming Restoration endured as the embodiment of the Han Chinese imperial legacy, bringing a close to the dynasty's four-century absence from the annals of power. Thus, the early 20th century would be remembered as a time when the Middle Kingdom, under a regency, sought to reconcile its ancient monarchical heritage with the contemporary milieu of a world in upheaval.

Chapter IX: The Reforged Pillars of the Middle Kingdom: Reinstating Jiàngmén, Shìshēn, and Zhūhóu

In the wake of the Xinhai Revolution and the fall of the Qing, the restoration of the Ming State, under Zhu Yuxun as Emperor Hongxian, heralded a return to China's classical heritage. This renewal of the monarchic tradition saw the rebirth of the Jiàngmén—the ancient knightly order imbued with a chivalric code—and revival of the imperial examination system that once empowered the Shìshēn class. Additionally, the reestablishment of the noble Zhūhóu paralleled the social structures of both the European aristocracies and the Japanese Kazoku, signifying a synthesis of indigenous traditions and modern influences.

Aided by the Empire of Japan—a nation that had undergone its own metamorphosis during the Meiji Restoration—the newly restored Ming government undertook an ambitious program to modernize China while preserving its traditional social orders. The bilateral relations with Japan were instrumental, offering both technical assistance and financial resources to propel the Middle Kingdom into a new era.

The Jiàngmén reflected an intriguing amalgamation of the ancient warrior ethos with modern martial pragmatism. Prominent among the new knights were individuals of remarkable valor and loyalty who had proven themselves during the turbulence of the early Republican period. In a society that revered its military heroes, these individuals embodied the ideal of service both to the throne and to the community.

In an effort to restore reverence for scholarly learning, the rigorous imperial examination system was reinstated, exalting the intellectual meritocracy symbolized by the Shìshēn. Those members of the mercantile class who excelled in these examinations were esteemed equally alongside those of age-old noble lineage, creating a conduit for social mobility and a class of officialdom unrivaled in its erudition.

The reinvigoration of the Zhūhóu offered a bridge to a nostalgic past. With the acknowledgment of primogeniture and hereditary titles, these noble families became patrons of the arts, guardians of culture, and an embodiment of continuity in an era of rapid transformation. Embracing aspects of the modern state such as constitutional governance and civic responsibility, the nobility became pillars of the restored empire, fostering stability amidst change.

The Dai Nippon Teikoku's strategic intent was manifold; their covert international diplomacy served to attract investment while promoting industrialization in the Heavenly Kingdom. The construction of nationwide railways and the development of education systems were crucial elements in this transformation. With Japanese guidance, China's economy burgeoned, and a burgeoning middle class emerged, educated and urbanized, increasingly detached from political squabbles of the past.

As these modern knights, scholars, and nobles assumed their roles within society, the once-prominent anti-Japanese factions within the Guohui began to crumble. Scandals of corruption, warlord affiliations, and mysterious disappearances plagued their ranks. The populace, now preoccupied with economic opportunities and societal advancement, largely ignored the political machinations of these discredited factions.

This era was thus characterized by an intricate balance where traditional orders were harmonized with modern necessities. China, under the guidance of the Ming restoration and the aegis of the Japanese Empire, entered a period of prosperity. The Middle Kingdom's transformation into a modern state rooted in the richness of its heritage became the cornerstone for both national pride and international status.

The rapport between the Empire of Japan and the resurgent Chinese kingdom served as a testament to the potential for cooperation between ancient foes, united in their shared aspiration for regional prominence. Together, they fostered a renaissance that buffered the Middle Kingdom against the fortunes of an uncertain world, setting a course for China well into the 20th century.

Chapter X: The Democratic Monarchy of the Ming - An Edifice of Paradox

In the historical narrative of China’s constitutional development, the post-Xinhai Revolution era under the restored Ming rule stands as one of the most compelling periods. It frequently defied orthodox categorization, melding seemingly irreconcilable systems of governance into a functioning polity—paradoxical though it may have seemed. With aspirations lain by the brave souls of the Xinhai Revolution against the Qing, the Ming re-emergence was concomitant with the embodiment of a state that was at once imperial and democratic, authoritative and libertous.

Under Emperor Hongxian, the newly-restored Ming State adopted a form of governance known as a constitutional parliamentary democratic imperial royal constitutional absolute monarchy. This structure was complex, blending the hierarchies and ceremonial allure of ancient monarchy with the liberal advancements of parliamentary governance. The contradiction, while stark, was reconciled through careful constitutional mechanisms and the auspices of a venerated Huaren monarchy, complemented by fully democratic processes. It marked a bold experimental synthesis.

Central to this system was the Guohui, the bicameral legislature composed of the Senate and the House of Representatives—seeds sowed during the nascent days of the Republic—now flourishing under the monarchical umbrella. The legislative body was styled to mirror those found in established constitutional monarchies, but wielded greater influence in the balancing act between the Huángdì's throne and the vox populi.

Elections—free, fair, and conducted by secret ballot—became a cornerstone of this newly-fashioned governance. The involvement of multiple parties in the intricate political ballet reflected the broad spectrum of political thought ranging from stalwart monarchists to reformist progressives. Their participation demonstrated genuine public will while bolstering the legitimacy of the Ming restoration.

Political campaigns were characterized by decorum and were less predicated on populist rhetoric than on the philosophies underpinning governance—theoretical discourses on the balance between autocracy and democracy. Policies largely reflected the middle path: advocating for modernization and social reform while preserving the rich tapestry of heritage.

The Guohui played a crucial role, deliberating and ratifying laws that would shape the Empire. Within its chambers, impassioned debates tackled economic policies, education reforms, and international relations. The balance of power tilted neither fully towards the Crown nor the Commons, but instead revolved around consensus and mutual accommodation.

The Crown, for its part, maintained its place as a symbol of national identity, unity, and stability. The reigning monarch's role was more than ceremonial; they held the prerogative to appoint the Prime Minister, traditionally the leader of the majority party or coalition. The Huángdì could, theoretically, dissolve the Guohui and call for fresh elections, though this power was seldom exercised, as political stability took precedent over royal interventionism.

The result was an intricate dance of political forces, where the vestiges of imperial grandeur integrated with the aspirations for popular sovereignty—a system that embellished the idea of a singular national narrative with democratic principles.

The great irony of the Ming democratic monarchy lay in its origins—the Xinhai Revolution against Manchu rule. The revolutionaries' primary goal was the establishment of a democratic republic free from the encumbrances of imperial despotism. Yet, the pendulum of history swung back to a variant of the entity they had opposed; an imperial entity resting upon the populist foundations they had fought to lay.

In this unique constitutional setup, the Middle Kingdom thrived. Growth in civic engagement and an expansion of civil liberties marked a significant step away from the autocratic past towards a more participative political culture, underpinned by the spiritual continuity of a rejuvenated albeit constitutionally reinvented Huaren monarchy. The Ming dynasty, thus reborn in the fire of revolution, arose not with an iron fist but with a gilded scepter rooted in the mandate of the people it governed.

Chapter XI: The Great Ming Army Reborn – The Revival of Ming Military Traditions under Modern Command

The post-Xinhai Revolution era witnessed the restoration of the Da Mingjun, a profound reinvigoration of the Ming Empire’s military ethos through the Beiyang Jun, evolving into the Anguojun, and finally manifesting as the Weihebudui. Each succession in this military lineage encapsulated a dedicated effort to reinstate the historical prowess of the Great Ming Army within a contemporary framework.

The Wei-Suo system, a legacy of the original Da Mingjun dating from the empire's inception in 1368 until its deterioration by 1662, had once been the backbone of the Ming military reach. Garrison-farming (Tuntian), which placed soldiers in frontier regions to turn untamed lands into thriving agricultural communities, not only ensured a logistical base for military campaigns but also promoted a sense of martial custodianship over the empire’s periphery. These battalions, while self-sufficient, rotated to the capital for specialized training, ensuring the central power remained deeply connected with its military extensions.

The Beiyang Jun, the Northern Ocean Army, first materialized as the Republic of China's foremost Western-style military formation. Its creation in the shadow of the Qing’s downfall arose from an urgency to defend the nascent republic. However, with the restoration of the Ming and the establishment of Anguojun, it underwent a transformation into an institution that served as the intermediary between past glory and future aspirations, bridging the traditional and modern.

With the advent of the Anguojun, the Republic channeled the ambitions of Beiyang into a nationalistic framework that sought to pacify internal dissent and solidify territorial control. Its stint, lasting from the mid-1920s until 1928, was an essential prelude to the final renaissance of the Ming military as the Weihebudui.

The role played by the Dai-Nippon Teikoku Rikugun cannot be understated. As China’s military protector and industrial mentor, Japan transferred critical knowledge and resources to its protégé. The profound impact of Japanese military training, strategy, equipment, and organization fostered a professional and capable Ming force. In particular, the Imperial Japanese Army’s role in this renewal process was crucial, invoking admiration and infusing a robust discipline into its counterpart.

The Weihebudui represented a culmination of centuries of military evolution. Under its banner, the remnants of the ancient Wei-Suo methodology found new life, as soldiers were once again connected to the land and their duty, albeit without relying on the old system of agricultural support. Instead, this reformed military body was supplied through modern logistics chains that mirrored the efficiency of global contemporaries.

The Weihebudui operated with an understanding of the past, but with eyes set firmly on future horizons. Embracing new technologies in arms and vehicle manufacturing—and applying innovative strategies and tactics gleaned from their Japanese allies—ensured the Ming State’s defense was formidable. In training, echoes of historical regimens persisted, although augmented by contemporary methods; Nanking, the Ming capital, once again became a locus of military acumen, both symbolic and strategic.

Thus, while the essence of the original Da Mingjun armored the spirit of the Weihebudui, it was the vision of a modern, nationalistic, and constitutional monarchy that galvanized the Ming Army’s rebirth—a unique fusion that honored tradition whilst adopting innovation. It was in this fertile era of revival that the Great Ming Army, steeped in heritage, reasserted itself as a vanguard of both the empire’s splendor and its sovereignty.

Chapter XII: The Shadow Guardians - Jinyiwei’s Restoration and the Imperial Surveillance Network

With the cultural and political resurrection of the Great Ming State after the Xinhai Revolution, the Ming saw not only the renewal of traditional military power but also the reestablishment of the secretive and omnipotent Jinyiwei—the Embroidered Uniform Guard. This elite unit, historically the emperor’s guardian and the dynasty’s enforcer, was restored as an integral component of the state’s internal security and intelligence apparatus, ensuring the protection of monarchical power and order within the burgeoning imperium.

Drawing inspiration from the Kempeitai—Japan’s formidable military police force with multifaceted functions from enforcing military laws to conducting counter-intelligence within the Dai Nippon Teikoku—the Ming’s restored Jinyiwei operated as a clandestine sword and shield. Its authority to bypass judicial norms for the swift prosecution of perceived enemies extended into a silent war against dissent, treachery, and espionage.

Reinstating the Jinyiwei required a careful blend of traditional practices and modern intelligence methodologies. The Ming sought Japanese expertise in this field, particularly from the Kempeitai, whose efficacy in maintaining stringent order and discipline was perceived as unmatched. Under the aegis of the Dai-Nippon Teikoku Rikugun, the newly restored Jinyiwei underwent a transformative phase, emerging as a fusion of covert operation agencies and a political commissariat.

Granted full autonomy, the Jinyiwei wielded considerable power. In times of peace, they were the state’s shadowy vigil—ever watchful, ever present, but seldom seen. Their network spanned the empire, and they could intercede in any matter, be it civil or military.

In war, they transitioned into political commissars within the Da Mingjun, emphasizing loyalty to the Ming throne. Their presence ensured the imperial edicts permeated every stratum of the military, combating subterfuge and promoting discipline. The Jinyiwei became not just political influencers within the ranks but also adepts in psychological warfare, disassembling enemy morale while fortifying the resolve of Ming soldiers.

The institution's restoration, therefore, signified more than an architectural remodeling of the state’s security apparatus—it was a statement of intent. The Jinyiwei’s omnipresence was the Empire’s guarantee of perpetual surveillance and control, its very re-establishment a confluence of ancient fears and modern statecraft.

As with much of the Ming restoration, the Jinyiwei’s revival occurred under the paradoxical sign of 'constitutional authoritarianism.' While the rule of law was held as a virtue in the burgeoning democracy, the Jinyiwei operated outside its boundaries. They were virtually unchecked by legal institutions that would otherwise stymie their function, and they thrived in the murky interstices of the Ming legal and political machine.

This clandestine force, however, was not solely an instrument of oppression. Its members, drawn from elite branches of society, were highly trained, unwaveringly devoted, and imbued with a keen understanding of their role as defenders of the dynastic order. They shared allegiance both to the Dragon Throne and to the safety and integrity of the Ming State, a dual loyalty manifest in their service and secrecy.

In the evolving landscape of Eastern Asia and the world, the Jinyiwei’s presence solidified the Ming’s determination to maintain a centralized imperial authority while transitioning into modernity. Rooted deeply in the heritage of its storied past, the Great Ming State thus entered a new epoch—a time where the old meets the new, and the guardians of the empire’s soul tread both in light and shadow.

Chapter XIII: The Manchurian Enclave: A New Chapter for the Qing Exiles

The aftermath of the Xinhai Revolution led to a significant territorial and political reconfiguration in East Asia, dominantly characterized by the emergence of the newly-restored Ming Dynasty in the heartland of China. Meanwhile, the former ruling house of the Qing, ousted by the revolutionary fervor, found refuge in Manchuria—a land of strategic importance and their ancestral home. This region, known in this era as Manjügo, offered the last bastion for the remnants of a once-great dynasty, now in exile yet seeking to redefine themselves in the throes of the 20th century.

Under the protective aegis of the Japanese Empire, Manjügo witnessed significant industrialization and infrastructural development as Japan sought to assert its influence over the strategic territory adjacent to its own imperial ambitions in the Korean Peninsula and broader Asian mainland. The Dai Nippon Teikoku played a pivotal role, effectively navigating Manjügo's transformation akin to a royal, imperial, and parliamentary democracy, reflective of European nation-states in the late 18th to early 20th centuries—albeit with an unmistakably Japanese imprint.

Manjügo's journey toward modernization accelerated post-1906, following the nation's stalwart defense of the Transamur region during the Russo-Japanese War. Staving off Russian interests in the contested territory, Manchuria emerged as a critical ally to Japan, reinforcing both their military alliance and reinforcing Japan's geopolitical position.

However, it was the Xinhai Revolution that crystallized Manjügo as an entity separate from Mainland China. From their seat in Mukden—now a burgeoning capital replete with fresh industry and Western influence—former Qing Royalty navigated their enclave amidst regional power plays. The Aisin-Gioro family transitioned into symbolic figures of stability and continuity, correlating their legitimacy with the parliamentary bodies that gave voice to Manjügo's population and elite.

With expertise and advisors from the Empire of Japan, Manjügo cultivated an environment of administrative efficiency and modern civil governance. A constitution delineating the balance between royal prerogatives and legislative power laid the foundations for this parliamentary democracy—a stark contrast to the nation's absolute monarchical past.

One profound area of influence remained the military, which, although undergoing its own modernization with Japanese assistance, retained ceremonial allegiances to the Qing lineage. The military served a dual function: safeguarding Manjügo's sovereignty and enforcing the political order, ensuring that parliamentary processes and the power of the crown remained unthreatened by external or domestic antagonists.

As Manjügo veered toward progress and modernity, it also grappled with the legacy of its Qing past. The social fabric bore a delicate weave: the preservation of Manchu identity amidst rapid economic development, urbanization, and cultural exchange. Education systems were overhauled, and technology adoption surged, resulting in a Manjügo that straddled between dynastic reverence and forward-looking innovation.

Japanese involvement was not altruistic; strategic interests and economic gains were deep-seated motives. Yet, the result of this interaction was undeniable: Manjügo, the erstwhile heartland of the Aisin-Gioro dominion, adapted to its new reality as a reformed and dynamic corner of Asia. Where once the absolute word of the Qing Emperor reigned supreme, now discourse within parliamentary halls dictated Manjügo's future—a future penned with an ink of change but imbued with indelible lines of its historic lineage.

Chapter XIV: Manjügo’s Ascension: The Pre-Manchukuo Imperium

As the pages of history turned following the seismic shifts of the Xinhai Revolution, the Qing Imperial Court found respite in the ancestral land of Manchuria—Manjügo. This period marked the genesis of a great experiment, where the culturally rich but politically exiled Qing Dynasty endeavored to reform itself under the influence and suzerainty of the Dai Nippon Teikoku, forging a polity reminiscent of Manchukuo decades prior to its historical appearance in the 1930s.

The Ding Si Restoration, occurring in the summer of 1917, set forth a movement led by Qing loyalists of the Ding Wu Army, who instigated a short-lived attempt to restore Puyi, the Last Emperor, to the throne in Beijing. Although this effort was quickly subdued in its incipiency, the spirit of revivalism continued to burn in the exiled hearts of Qing diehards.

In contrast to the actual timeline, where Zhang Zuolin, the warlord of Manchuria, and his infamous son, Zhang Xueliang, presided over the territory with an iron fist, this alternate history portrayed a divergent path. Both father and son remained integral members of the Ding Wu Army, a group dedicated to the preservation and restoration of Qing governance.

Zhang Xueliang, known in history as the "Young Marshal," is reimagined here not as a power-hungry military autocrat but as a dedicated protege, groomed by the Japanese Imperial Army and the Kempeitai to succeed the nationalist icon, Dr. Sun Yat-sen, as the Prime Minister of the Great Ming Interior upon the latter's demise.

Under the guiding hand of Japan, Manjügo experienced rapid change towards becoming a constitutional democratic monarchy. The Japanese influence was central to Manjügo’s development. The symbiotic relationship saw the Aisin-Gioro family regaining a semblance of control while enshrining a form of constitutionalism that curtailed the absolutism of their past. 

Empowered by a new parliamentary structure, Manjügo radiated the early outlines of what would eventually become Manchukuo, with an imperial figurehead presiding over a modern administrative state. The transition bore the hallmarks of an entity balancing the line between maintaining royal dignity and embracing democratic ideals.

Japan's role was multifaceted—though acting as a protector and mentor, it also ensured that Manjügo’s economic and strategic interests dovetailed neatly with its own imperial policies. Seoul and Tokyo provided the blueprint for political organization, economic infrastructure, and societal reform that allowed Manjügo to stride towards a future as a developed nation, akin to the European powers of the age.

The result was a unique cultural and political entity that cultivated its Manchurian heritage while implementing Japanese-inspired modernization and parliamentary democracy. This process enabled the Qing royalty to reassert a degree of influence in the region, not as absolute monarchs but as constitutional figures within a broader democratic framework.

In retrospect, Manjügo’s renaissance period laid down the early imperatives for the conceptualization and implementation of what later became Manchukuo, albeit under different historical circumstances. It witnessed a former Qing dynasty, modernized yet still vested with imperial traditions, an Asian empire molded but not consumed by its Japanese alliance. Thus, Manjügo—once teetering on the fringes of dynastic collapse—managed to reclaim a semblance of its old glory in a new epoch, through political accommodation and the nurturing support of Japan.

Chapter XV: The Pacific Overture: Dai Nippon’s Alignment with Central Powers

The Weltkrieg, or the Great War as it is variously known, was a planetary conflagration that, in our alternate history, witnessed the participation of Dai Nippon Teikoku alongside the Central Powers: a coalition comprising the German Empire, the Austro-Hungarian Empire, the Bulgarian Tsardom, and the Ottoman Empire. This profound reimagining of the conflict reshaped geopolitical boundaries and heralded a new order across continents.

Dai Nippon Teikoku, through fortuitous circumstances driven by the Zimmerman Telegram's interception, forged an unprecedented alliance with die Mittelmächte (the Central Powers). Far from the historic reality wherein Japan sided with the Entente, this timeline sees it pivoting due to assurances and territorial promises from the German Empire, forging a path alongside Berlin, Vienna, Sofia, and Constantinople, driven by mutual interests and imperial aspirations.

The German Empire, at the heart of Central Europe, was the Ikarian arch that united the ambit of the Central Powers. Governed by the House of Hohenzollern and combining the offices of Kaiser and Reichspräsident, the German polity exemplified militarism married to constitutional powers. The war's progress demonstrated German preeminence in military strategy and statecraft, setting the stage for a reshaped Europe under Berlin's helm.

Austria-Hungary, the kaleidoscopic empire of dual monarchy, found renewed vigor as existential threats were quelled through the establishment of Serbian and Montenegrin satellite states. The House of Habsburg-Lorraine presided over a patchwork of nationalities striving for dominion in the hotly contested Balkans, tirelessly working to extinguish the flames of Yugoslav nationalism.

In Eastern Europe, the emergence of the Bulgarian Tsardom under the reign of the House of Saxe-Coburg and Gotha marked the advent of a new Roman revival, invigorating Bulgarian imperial ambitions. The Ottomans, too, saw retention and expansion of their territories in a post-war Middle Eastern landscape uncarved by the Sykes-Picot Agreement.

The Weltkrieg's end was precipitated by the capitulation of the United Kingdom following the German march on Paris, the Italian exit from the war, and the Bolshevik Revolution in Russia. The Treaty of Brest-Litovsk allowed for German and Austro-Hungarian expansion and client states in the former Russian Empire's frontiers.

In the process, the German Empire and Austria-Hungary carved spheres of influence across the war-beaten landscapes of Europe. The United Baltic Duchy and the Kingdom of Poland emerged as German-affiliated realms, while Finland and Belarus aligned with both German and Austro-Hungarian patronage. The Austrian monarchy also restored Serbia and facilitated a Montenegrin satellite state, quenching fires of Slavic nationalism with imperial subjugation.

The Ottoman triumph resulted in the prevalence of the Imperial House of Osman over a robust expanse of territories, firmly establishing the Devlet-i ʿAlīye-i ʿOsmānīye as the leading Islamic power, reining from Constantinople to the Arabian sands and standing sentinel over the Eastern Roman legacy.

For Dai Nippon Teikoku, the spoils of Weltkrieg were transformative, with territorial acquisitions in the Russian Far East, turning Siberia into a confederacy of indigenous tribes, and cementing its preeminence in East Asia with the liberation of Vietnam, Cambodia, Laos from French colonial rule.

The consequence of this global realignment was the dawn of a new order; Central European powers ascended, reconfiguring the classic balance of power on the continent. Simultaneously, the Pacific witnessed the rise of Dai Nippon Teikoku, becoming not merely a regional but a global power, setting the stage for further developments in an increasingly interconnected and fractious world stage in the years to come.

Chapter XVI: The Fractured Bear: Intervention in the Russian Civil War and the Ascendance of Kolchak

The vicissitudes of the Great War extended beyond the Armistice into the tumult of the Russian Civil War, a conflict that underscored a global struggle between royalist traditions and revolutionary ideologies. This period highlights the fractured narratives and varying alliances that defined post-Weltkrieg geopolitics, particularly the involvement of die Mittelmächte and their intention to preserve monarchist rule against the surging tide of Bolshevism.

The Russian Civil War erupted against the backdrop of a world weary of warfare, yet the red specter of Bolshevism presented a dire menace that unified disparate forces under a banner of royalist resurgence. The Bolshevik uprising, which commenced on November 7, 1917, threatened the Imperial Russian fabric and pitted Lenin's red revolutionaries against the staunch White Movement loyal to the Romanov legacy.

In an ironic historical turn, the British, emerging worn from the Weltkrieg stalemate, harbored clandestine motives to stifle the Central Powers by indirectly bolstering Bolshevik forces, contributing to the instability of the Mittelmächte’s Eastern flank. This gambit aimed to exhaust Mittelmächte resources and divert their focus from European consolidation.

However, the British ploy faltered. The White Movement, though internally disparate, found resolve through foreign support – a coalition of French, Italian, German, and Austro-Hungarian aid characterized by material support, advisors, and volunteers. This potent backing, alongside the vestiges of Russian patriotism, fortified the White Movement, allowing it to gain the upper hand in a war of attrition against the Bolshevik tide.

The White victory was predominantly marked by the emergence of Admiral Alexander Kolchak as the Supreme Leader of Russia. A respected naval officer and an emblem of Russian unity, Kolchak's ascendancy to leadership came after the tragic assassinations of the Romanovs – a decimation carried out by a brutal Bolshevik onslaught. With their demise, Kolchak, bolstered by Mittelmächte support, became the bulwark against the red tide, standing firm where the regal line had faltered.

In the post-civil war Russian state, grappled by economic devastation and political fragmentation, Kolchak's rule transpired as a period of reconstitution. Yet the new Russia unfurled as a quasi-autonomous realm, with the shadow of Vienna and Berlin cast firmly upon it. The newly formed Russian governance drew heavily from both Hohenzollern and Habsburg influences, with profoundly Germanic and Austro-Hungarian attributes in its bureaucratic and military infrastructures.

Die Mittelmächte, having intervened decisively in the Russian Civil War, now envisioned a future Russian monarchy aligned with Central Powers politics. Discussions brewed between Berlin and Vienna regarding the installation of either a Hohenzollern or Habsburg scion upon the Russian throne, contingent upon Kolchak's eventual relinquishment of power or demise—a testament to their overarching designs to hem in the Bolshevik threat and extend their royal networks.

Aligned by the shared aim of upholding monarchical systems amid a world increasingly enamored with populist revolutions, Mittelmächte and the remnants of the French and Italian monarchists found congruity in their involvement in White Russia. Through this alliance, die Mittelmächte reasserted the prowess of royalty against revolution, positioning Kolchak as a stopgap figurehead whilst laying the groundwork for a stabilizing imperial insertion once the mantle of leadership transitioned.

Thus, White Russia emerged as a nation held in equipoise—steadying after a brutal conflict, enduring with the lifeline from powers vested in the old world order, and steadying its gaze upon the ostensibly serene yesteryears of Romanov rule, now a mere whisper in the annals of a world unalterably altered.
